title: Going Further
text: For many historians, the free tiers of ChatGPT and Google Gemini will be sufficient for occasional use: a few transcription sessions, some translation help, the odd coding question. If you use these tools a few times a week for modest tasks, you may never need to pay for anything.

title: Going Further
text: For many historians, the free tiers of ChatGPT and Google Gemini will be sufficient for occasional use: a few transcription sessions, some translation help, the odd coding question. If you use these tools a few times a week for modest tasks, you may never need to pay for anything.

title: Going Further
text:
When Free Is Not Enough
You are likely to hit the limits of free tools if you:

Process large volumes of documents. Free tiers have daily usage caps. A sustained transcription or translation project will exhaust them quickly.
Work with long documents. Free models often have smaller context windows — they can only process a limited amount of text at once. If you need to feed in a 30-page chapter or a long set of notes, you may need a paid tier.
Need the most capable models. The best-performing models are typically reserved for paid subscribers. The difference is noticeable for complex tasks like nuanced translation, sophisticated code generation, or working with messy data.
Want to upload files. Some free tiers restrict file uploads (PDFs, images, spreadsheets). Paid tiers generally allow this.

title: Going Further
text:
Paid Options
Prices and capabilities change frequently. The following is accurate as of early 2026 but should be verified before subscribing.

ChatGPT Plus (~$20 USD/month)

Access to the most capable OpenAI models
Higher usage limits
File uploads (PDFs, images, spreadsheets)
Custom instructions that persist across conversations
Web browsing and image generation

Best for: General-purpose use across transcription, translation, and writing tasks. The most widely used option, with the largest ecosystem of guides and tutorials.


Claude Pro (~$20 USD/month)

Access to Anthropic’s Claude models (Sonnet, Opus)
Larger context window — Claude can process significantly longer documents in a single conversation (up to ~150 pages of text)
File uploads, including large PDFs
Careful, detailed responses; tends to be more cautious about stating things it is unsure of

Best for: Working with long documents, detailed transcription, and tasks where you value accuracy and caution over speed. Claude’s longer context window is a meaningful advantage for historians working with multi-page sources.


Google Gemini Advanced (~$20 USD/month, bundled with Google One AI Premium)

Access to Google’s most capable models
Integration with Google Workspace (Docs, Sheets, Gmail)
Large context window

Best for: Historians already embedded in the Google ecosystem who want AI assistance across their existing workflow.


Claude Code (Subscription or usage-based)
Claude Code is a different kind of tool. It runs in your computer’s terminal (command line) rather than in a web browser, and it can read, write, and edit files on your computer directly. It is designed for extended, multi-step tasks rather than quick questions.
What it can do that a chatbot cannot:

Read an entire project directory — your chapter drafts, your data files, your scripts — and work with them as a whole.
Write and run code directly on your machine, testing it and fixing errors automatically.
Edit your files in place, with your review and approval.
Maintain context across long work sessions.

Why a historian might want this:

Building and maintaining a database (e.g., loading census data into Neo4j, cleaning a large spreadsheet)
Processing a batch of digitized documents
Writing and debugging data analysis scripts
Proofreading a multi-chapter manuscript with consistent style guidelines

Claude Code is the most powerful option discussed in this guide but also the one with the steepest learning curve. It requires basic comfort with the command line. For historians doing computationally intensive research, the investment in learning it can pay off substantially.

title: Going Further
section: Do You Need Any of This?
text:
Do You Need Any of This?
Honestly — maybe not. If you are writing a traditional monograph based on archival research, and your sources are in languages you read fluently, and you do not work with large datasets, the free tier of ChatGPT or Gemini may serve you well for the occasional task. The paid tools are most valuable for researchers whose work involves:

Large-scale document processing
Sources in multiple languages
Computational methods (databases, text analysis, mapping)
Long and complex writing projects

Start with the free tools. If you find yourself bumping against their limits regularly, a $20/month subscription is a modest investment for a professional research tool.

title: Going Further
text:
A Note on Privacy
When you use a web-based LLM, the text you provide is sent to the company’s servers. For most research tasks this is not a concern, but be aware:

Do not paste unpublished, embargoed, or confidential material into a free-tier chatbot without understanding the service’s data policy.
Most paid tiers offer options to opt out of having your conversations used for model training. Check the settings.
If you are working with sensitive data (e.g., records involving living individuals), consult your institution’s research ethics guidelines.

title: Research Tasks
text: This section walks through specific research tasks where GenAI tools have proven useful to working historians. Each subsection includes sample prompts you can adapt. The emphasis throughout is on using the tool to accelerate mechanical work while keeping the interpretive judgment in your hands.

title: Research Tasks
text: This section walks through specific research tasks where GenAI tools have proven useful to working historians. Each subsection includes sample prompts you can adapt. The emphasis throughout is on using the tool to accelerate mechanical work while keeping the interpretive judgment in your hands.

title: Research Tasks
text:
Transcription

The Use Case
You have digitized images of manuscripts, printed documents, or typescripts that need to be converted to searchable, editable text. Traditional OCR (optical character recognition) struggles with handwriting, damaged pages, and pre-twentieth-century typefaces. LLMs with vision capabilities (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) can often do better because they use context — they understand that a colonial dispatch is likely to contain certain words and phrases, which helps them read ambiguous characters.


How to Do It

Upload an image of the document page to the chat interface. Most current tools accept image files (JPG, PNG) and PDFs.
Provide context in your prompt:


This is a page from a handwritten letter written by a Hudson’s Bay Company factor at York Factory, circa 1820. Please transcribe the text as accurately as possible. Preserve original spelling, capitalization, and punctuation. Where you are uncertain about a word, mark it with [?].


Review the output against the original image. Correct errors and ask follow-up questions:


In line 7, you transcribed “provisions” — I think the original reads “previsions.” Can you look at that word again?



Tips

Provide as much context as you can: the document type, date, language, and any distinctive features (e.g., “this writer uses the long s”).
Process one page at a time for best results.
For large-scale transcription projects (hundreds of pages), specialized OCR tools like Transkribus may be more efficient. LLMs are best for small batches or particularly difficult pages.

title: Research Tasks
text:
Translation

The Use Case
You are working with primary sources in a language you can read with difficulty, or a language where you need to process many documents quickly. You do not need a publication-quality translation — you need a working draft that gets you close enough to assess the source’s relevance and content.


How to Do It

Please translate the following passage from 18th-century French into English. This is from a colonial administrator’s report on trade in the Antilles. Preserve the meaning as closely as possible; I do not need an elegant literary translation. Flag any terms that are ambiguous or that you are uncertain about.
[paste the French text here]



Tips

Always compare the translation against the original for key passages you plan to cite.
LLMs handle French, Spanish, German, Dutch, and Latin well. Performance degrades for less-represented languages and for heavily abbreviated or formulaic text (e.g., notarial records).
For period-specific vocabulary, tell the model the era: “This is 17th-century legal French; habitant here means settler-farmer, not inhabitant.”
If you plan to quote a translated passage in a publication, have the translation verified independently. LLM translation is a research aid, not a citable source.

title: Research Tasks
text:
Literature Discovery

The Use Case
You are beginning a new project or entering an unfamiliar historiography and want to identify key works, major debates, and starting points for reading.


How to Do It

I am starting a research project on the role of insurance in the British slave trade, roughly 1700–1807. Can you identify the key secondary works I should read? I am particularly interested in the intersection of financial history and the history of slavery.



The Critical Caveat
LLMs frequently hallucinate citations. They generate titles, authors, and publication details that look correct but do not correspond to any real publication. This is one of the most dangerous failure modes for historians.
Rules for using LLM-generated reading lists:

Treat every citation as a suggestion to verify, not a confirmed reference.
Search for each title in your library catalogue, JSTOR, or Google Scholar before adding it to your bibliography.
If a title does not turn up in any database, it probably does not exist.
The model is better at identifying well-known works than obscure articles. Use it for orientation, not for comprehensive literature review.



Tips

Ask for the bibliographic details explicitly: “Please include the full title, author, publisher, and date for each work.”
Cross-reference with review essays, Oxford Bibliographies, or historiographic surveys for the field.
Consider this a complement to, not a replacement for, searching databases yourself.

title: Research Tasks
text:
Coding and Data Management

The Use Case
You have data — a spreadsheet of census records, a collection of digitized documents, a database of shipping records — and you need to clean, transform, analyse, or visualize it. You are not a programmer, or you are a beginning programmer who gets stuck on syntax.
This is one of the areas where LLMs provide the most dramatic productivity gains for historians. Tasks that might take a week to learn from scratch can often be accomplished in an afternoon with LLM assistance.


How to Do It

I have a CSV file with 428 rows of data about Saskatchewan settlements from the 1921 census. The columns include settlement name, founding year, population, railway company, and year of railway arrival. Some cells contain multiple values separated by semicolons (e.g., a settlement served by two railway companies). I want to write a Python script that:

Reads the CSV file
Splits the multi-value cells into separate rows
Creates a summary table showing, for each railway company, how many settlements it served and the average population of those settlements
Exports the result to a new CSV file

Please write the script and explain each step so I can understand what it does.



Tips

Be specific about your data: describe the columns, the format, any irregularities.
Ask the model to explain the code, not just write it. Understanding what the script does allows you to modify it later and catch errors.
Always test the code on your actual data. The model may make assumptions about your file structure that are not correct.
Start with small, well-defined tasks. Once you are comfortable, you can tackle larger data projects.
If the first attempt does not work, paste the error message back into the conversation. The model is usually good at diagnosing and fixing its own mistakes.



What This Looks Like in Practice
A historian building a database of plantation estates might use an LLM to:

Write a script that scrapes structured data from a website
Clean inconsistent date formats across thousands of records
Generate Cypher queries to load data into a Neo4j graph database
Create visualizations of temporal patterns (e.g., settlement founding dates by decade)

None of this requires the historian to become a software engineer. It requires them to describe clearly what they want and to verify that the output is correct — skills that transfer directly from historical method.

title: Research Tasks
text:
Organizing and Structuring Notes

The Use Case
You have extensive research notes, archival transcriptions, or document summaries and want to organize them by theme, date, or topic.


How to Do It

I have transcribed notes from 30 letters written by colonial administrators in Jamaica between 1815 and 1834. Each note is a paragraph summarizing the letter’s contents with the date and author. I am going to paste all 30 summaries below. Please organize them chronologically and identify the major recurring themes across the correspondence.



Tips

This works best when you have already done the intellectual work of reading and summarizing. The LLM organizes your notes; it does not generate understanding for you.
Useful for conference paper preparation, chapter outlining, or identifying gaps in your research.

title: Deep Research Tools
text:
Several GenAI products now offer a “deep research” or “research” mode that goes beyond a simple chatbot response. Instead of answering your question from memory, these tools search the web, read multiple sources, synthesize what they find, and produce a structured report — often with citations and footnotes.
The major options as of early 2026:

Gemini Deep Research (Google) — available with Gemini Advanced ($20/month). Searches the web, reads pages, and produces multi-page reports with source links.
ChatGPT Deep Research (OpenAI) — available with ChatGPT Plus and Pro. Similar approach: extended web browsing, synthesis, and structured output.
Perplexity (perplexity.ai) — available in a free tier with limits, and a Pro tier (~$20/month). Designed from the ground up as a research tool. Every claim is linked to a source. The free tier is a good starting point.
Claude with web search (Anthropic) — Claude Pro can search the web when asked. Less automated than the dedicated deep research tools, but the underlying model is strong on synthesis and nuance.

These tools represent a genuine step forward from the basic chatbot experience described in earlier sections. They can produce results in minutes that would take hours of manual searching. They are also where GenAI’s limitations become most consequential for historians.

title: Deep Research Tools
text:
Several GenAI products now offer a “deep research” or “research” mode that goes beyond a simple chatbot response. Instead of answering your question from memory, these tools search the web, read multiple sources, synthesize what they find, and produce a structured report — often with citations and footnotes.
The major options as of early 2026:

Gemini Deep Research (Google) — available with Gemini Advanced ($20/month). Searches the web, reads pages, and produces multi-page reports with source links.
ChatGPT Deep Research (OpenAI) — available with ChatGPT Plus and Pro. Similar approach: extended web browsing, synthesis, and structured output.
Perplexity (perplexity.ai) — available in a free tier with limits, and a Pro tier (~$20/month). Designed from the ground up as a research tool. Every claim is linked to a source. The free tier is a good starting point.
Claude with web search (Anthropic) — Claude Pro can search the web when asked. Less automated than the dedicated deep research tools, but the underlying model is strong on synthesis and nuance.

These tools represent a genuine step forward from the basic chatbot experience described in earlier sections. They can produce results in minutes that would take hours of manual searching. They are also where GenAI’s limitations become most consequential for historians.

title: Deep Research Tools
text:
What They Do Well

Rapid Orientation in an Unfamiliar Field
If you are beginning a project in a historiography you do not know, a deep research tool can produce a useful map of the terrain in fifteen minutes. Ask:

I am a historian beginning a research project on the role of marine insurance in the British Atlantic slave trade, roughly 1700–1807. Please search for the key secondary literature and major scholarly debates. I am interested in work published since 2000, particularly at the intersection of financial history and the history of slavery. Provide full bibliographic details for each source and a brief summary of its argument.

The result will typically be a structured report identifying major scholars, key monographs and articles, and the main lines of debate. This is genuinely useful as a starting point — comparable to asking a knowledgeable colleague for reading suggestions, but faster and more systematic.


Finding Recent Work
Deep research tools that search the web can surface recent publications that may not yet appear in standard bibliographic databases. For fast-moving fields or recent special issues, this is a real advantage over traditional database searching.


Identifying Connections Across Fields
Because these tools search broadly, they sometimes surface relevant work in adjacent fields that a historian searching within disciplinary databases might miss — economics journals, legal history, historical geography, environmental science.


Producing Working Bibliographies
The best deep research reports include enough bibliographic detail that you can quickly verify each source in your library catalogue or JSTOR. This makes them useful as a triage tool: generate a long list, verify what exists, and then decide what to read.

title: Deep Research Tools
section: Where They Fail — and Why It Matters More Here
text:
Where They Fail — and Why It Matters More Here
The limitations described in the Limitations section apply to deep research tools with particular force, because the output looks more authoritative. A chatbot response that gets something wrong is easy to spot — it reads like a casual answer. A deep research report with footnotes, structured sections, and scholarly language is much harder to evaluate critically. The very features that make these tools useful also make their failures more dangerous.

Mixed Real and Fabricated Citations
This is the central problem. Deep research reports typically contain a mixture of:

Real sources, correctly cited and accurately summarized.
Real sources, cited but inaccurately summarized or mischaracterized.
Fabricated sources that do not exist, presented with plausible-sounding titles, authors, journal names, and dates.

The fabricated sources are not flagged. They appear in the same footnote format as the real ones. The ratio varies — some reports are largely accurate, others contain multiple fabrications — but you cannot tell in advance which kind you have received.
This means every single citation in a deep research report must be independently verified. This is not a suggestion; it is a requirement. A historian who cites a source from a deep research report without checking that it exists risks citing a work that was never published.


Confident Mischaracterization
Even when a source is real, the tool may misrepresent its argument. A nuanced article that complicates a received narrative may be summarized as if it simply confirms it. A book that is primarily about one topic may be cited for a claim it makes only in passing. The tool does not distinguish between a work’s central thesis and an incidental observation.


Historiographic Flattening
Deep research tools tend to present scholarly debates as settled when they are not, or to smooth over genuine disagreements. A report on the “Williams thesis” might present it as either confirmed or refuted, when the actual historiographic situation is far more complicated. The tools do not handle ambiguity, ongoing debate, or productive disagreement well.


Source Quality Blindness
These tools do not distinguish between a peer-reviewed article in Past & Present and a blog post. They weight sources by accessibility and relevance to the query, not by scholarly authority. A deep research report may cite high-quality scholarship alongside popular histories, journalism, or Wikipedia summaries without distinguishing between them.

title: Deep Research Tools
text:
A Practical Workflow
Given these strengths and limitations, here is a workflow that gets the most value from deep research tools while managing the risks:

Step 1: Generate
Run a deep research query on your topic. Be specific about what you want:

Search for peer-reviewed scholarship published since 2010 on [your topic]. Focus on [specific aspects]. Provide full bibliographic details (author, title, journal/publisher, date, volume, pages) for each source.



Step 2: Verify Every Citation
Go through the report’s citations one by one:

Search for each title in your library catalogue, Google Scholar, JSTOR, or WorldCat.
If a source does not appear in any database, it probably does not exist. Remove it.
If a source exists, check that the author, date, and publication details match.

This step is non-negotiable. Budget time for it.


Step 3: Check Summaries Against Originals
For sources you plan to use, read at least the abstract and introduction yourself. Verify that the tool’s summary accurately reflects the work’s argument. Do not rely on the tool’s characterization.


Step 4: Fill the Gaps
Deep research reports are biased toward digitally accessible, English-language sources. After verifying what the tool found, search manually for:

Work in other languages relevant to your topic.
Older foundational texts that may not be well-represented online.
Archival sources and primary source collections (which these tools cannot access).
Recent work that may not yet be indexed.



Step 5: Read
There is no substitute for this. The deep research report tells you what might be worth reading. The reading itself is your job.

title: Deep Research Tools
text:
Comparing the Tools
Each deep research tool has different strengths. Based on early 2026 capabilities:








Tool
Strengths
Weaknesses




Perplexity
Source links for every claim; free tier available; good for quick factual queries
Can be shallow on historiographic nuance; free tier has limits


Gemini Deep Research
Excellent on well-documented historiography; thorough multi-page reports
Degrades badly at the edges of knowledge (see below)


ChatGPT Deep Research
Strong synthesis; good at identifying scholarly debates
Citation accuracy varies; can hallucinate convincingly


Claude (web search)
More cautious; better at flagging uncertainty and acknowledging gaps
Weaker on historiography; less automated; requires more guidance



These assessments will change as the tools improve. The verification workflow remains the same regardless of which tool you use.

A Note on Gemini and the Limits of Knowledge
Gemini Deep Research deserves special mention because it illustrates a pattern that applies to all these tools but is particularly visible here. When you ask Gemini about a well-established historiographic debate — the Williams thesis, the standard of living debate, the “great divergence” — it is genuinely impressive. It identifies the key scholars, maps the lines of argument, and produces reports that a graduate student would find useful.
The problem emerges when you push toward questions at the frontier of historical knowledge — topics where the primary sources are thin, not digitized, or simply do not exist. When Gemini cannot find strong evidence, it does not say so. Instead, it answers the question anyway, drawing on whatever it can find: sources from the wrong decade, tangentially related evidence, or speculative claims from weak secondary sources presented with the same confidence as established scholarship. The report reads just as polished and authoritative as one about a well-documented topic. The difference is invisible unless you already know enough to spot it.
This is the fundamental trap of deep research tools: they are most useful when you know the least about a topic, and least reliable in exactly that situation. When you are entering an unfamiliar field — the very moment you most need guidance — you are also least equipped to evaluate what the tool gives you. The verification workflow described above is the only safeguard.


A Note on Claude and the Limits of Training Data
Claude presents roughly the opposite trade-off from Gemini. Because Anthropic likely had less access to paywalled academic journals and monographs in its training data, Claude is noticeably weaker on established historiography — it may miss key works or be vague where Gemini is specific. On the other hand, Claude is better at recognizing and communicating its own uncertainty. When it cannot find strong evidence for a claim, it is more likely to say so rather than filling the gap with weak or mismatched sources.
This is a genuine advantage, but it is not absolute. Claude will still sometimes present uncertain claims with more confidence than they deserve. The difference is one of degree: you are somewhat less likely to receive a confident answer built on evidence from the wrong decade, and somewhat more likely to receive an honest “I am not sure about this.” For a historian, a tool that says “I don’t know” is often more useful than one that gives you a plausible-sounding wrong answer — but you should still verify everything regardless of which tool you use.

title: Deep Research Tools
section: When Deep Research Tools Are Most Useful
text:
When Deep Research Tools Are Most Useful

Entering a new field. You are starting a project outside your usual area and need a map of the literature.
Checking for recent work. You have not searched a particular topic in a year or two and want to see what has been published.
Interdisciplinary questions. Your project touches on economics, law, environmental science, or another field, and you want to find relevant work outside history journals.
Generating leads, not conclusions. You treat the output as a list of things to check, not as a finished literature review.

title: Deep Research Tools
text:
When They Are Not Useful

Your own specialty. You almost certainly know the literature better than the tool does. It may surface the occasional overlooked article, but the risk of fabricated or mischaracterized sources is not worth the time for a field you already command.
Archival research. These tools cannot access unpublished sources, manuscript collections, or finding aids.
Historiographic argument. A literature review in a thesis or article must reflect your reading and your interpretation of the field. A deep research report is a starting point for that work, not a substitute for it.

title: Deep Research Tools
text:
The Bottom Line
Deep research tools are the most impressive and the most dangerous GenAI application for historians. They can save you hours of initial searching. They can also fill your bibliography with sources that do not exist and your notes with summaries that misrepresent the scholarship. The tool does the searching. The verification, the reading, and the thinking are yours.

title: Getting Started
text:
You can begin with either of these free options. Both require only an email address to create an account:

ChatGPT (chatgpt.com) — Made by OpenAI. The free tier gives you access to the GPT-4o model with some usage limits. This is probably the most widely used LLM.
Google Gemini (gemini.google.com) — Made by Google. Free with a Google account. Comparable capability to ChatGPT for most tasks.

Both tools work in a web browser. You type a message, the model responds, and you can continue the conversation. There is nothing to install.
For this guide, the examples use ChatGPT, but the prompts work in any tool with minor adjustments.

title: Getting Started
text:
You can begin with either of these free options. Both require only an email address to create an account:

ChatGPT (chatgpt.com) — Made by OpenAI. The free tier gives you access to the GPT-4o model with some usage limits. This is probably the most widely used LLM.
Google Gemini (gemini.google.com) — Made by Google. Free with a Google account. Comparable capability to ChatGPT for most tasks.

Both tools work in a web browser. You type a message, the model responds, and you can continue the conversation. There is nothing to install.
For this guide, the examples use ChatGPT, but the prompts work in any tool with minor adjustments.

title: Getting Started
text:
Your First Conversation
Open one of the tools above and try the following prompt. Copy and paste it, or adapt it to a topic you know well:

I am a historian studying the development of the western Canadian grain trade in the early twentieth century. Can you give me a brief overview of the major developments between 1900 and 1930, focusing on the role of grain elevators and the Winnipeg Grain Exchange?

Read the response. Some of it will be accurate. Some of it may be vague or slightly wrong. Notice:

Does it cite specific sources? (Probably not, or if it does, check whether they exist.)
Does it capture the broad outlines correctly?
Where does it feel generic — like a textbook summary rather than a historian’s analysis?

This is a useful calibration exercise. You now have a sense of what the tool knows and does not know about a topic you understand.

title: Getting Started
text:
A More Useful Exercise: Transcription
LLMs are often more useful for concrete tasks than for open-ended questions. Try this:

Find a page from a digitized historical document — a handwritten letter, a printed government report with difficult typography, or a census page. If you have a PDF on your computer, many LLM tools now allow you to upload images or documents directly.
Upload or paste the text and ask:


Please transcribe the text in this image as accurately as possible. This is a [type of document] from [approximate date and context]. Preserve the original spelling and punctuation.


Compare the output against the original. Where does it succeed? Where does it struggle?

For printed text in good condition, LLMs are often very accurate. For handwriting, the results are more variable but can still be a useful first pass that you then correct.

title: Getting Started
text:
Understanding the Conversation Model
A few things are worth knowing about how these tools work in practice:
Context window. The model can only “remember” a certain amount of text within a single conversation. If your conversation becomes very long, the model may lose track of things you said earlier. For long tasks, it can help to start a new conversation and restate the key context.
System prompts and instructions. You can begin a conversation by telling the model what role you want it to play or what constraints to follow. For example:

You are helping me transcribe 19th-century French colonial correspondence. The documents are from the Archives nationales d’outre-mer. Please preserve the original French text and flag any words you are uncertain about with [?].

This kind of framing improves results significantly.
Follow-up questions. You can refine the model’s output by asking follow-up questions in the same conversation:

That transcription looks mostly right, but I think the word you read as “commerce” in line 3 is actually “commode.” Can you look at that section again?

The model will adjust. This iterative back-and-forth is often more productive than trying to get a perfect result on the first try.

title: Getting Started
text:
What You Have Learned
After fifteen minutes with a free tool, you should have a sense of:

How the conversational interface works
What kind of output the model produces for topics you know
How transcription assistance works in practice
The importance of checking everything

The next section, Research Tasks, walks through specific applications in more detail.

title: Copy Editing
text:
There is a difference between asking an LLM to write for you and asking it to edit what you have already written. This guide is about the latter. The assumption is that you have drafted your own prose — the arguments, the structure, the evidence are yours — and you want help catching errors, tightening sentences, and improving clarity.
This is not meaningfully different from asking a colleague to read a draft, or from using Grammarly, or from working with a copy editor. The tool is more capable than a spell-checker and less capable than a good human editor. It is useful precisely in that middle range.

title: Copy Editing
text:
There is a difference between asking an LLM to write for you and asking it to edit what you have already written. This guide is about the latter. The assumption is that you have drafted your own prose — the arguments, the structure, the evidence are yours — and you want help catching errors, tightening sentences, and improving clarity.
This is not meaningfully different from asking a colleague to read a draft, or from using Grammarly, or from working with a copy editor. The tool is more capable than a spell-checker and less capable than a good human editor. It is useful precisely in that middle range.

title: Copy Editing
text:
How to Do It

Basic Proofreading
Paste a section of your draft and ask:

Please proofread the following passage for spelling, grammar, and punctuation errors. Do not change the content, argument, or voice — only flag mechanical errors. List each error with the original text and the suggested correction.

This produces a list of corrections you can accept or reject individually, rather than a rewritten passage that you have to compare line by line.


Clarity and Flow

Please read the following passage and suggest improvements to clarity and readability. Do not change the argument or the evidence. Focus on sentence structure, word choice, and flow. Present your suggestions as a list of specific changes, not a rewritten version.



Consistency Checking

I am writing a chapter that uses the following terms: “enslaved person” (not “slave”), “slaveholder” (not “slave owner”), and “plantation” (not “estate” except when referring to legal documents). Please check the following passage for consistency with these preferences and flag any deviations.



Citation and Footnote Review

Please check the following passage for any footnotes that appear to reference the wrong source, or for claims that are made without a supporting footnote. I have pasted the passage with its footnotes below.

title: Copy Editing
text:
Staying in Control
The key risk with AI-assisted editing is that the tool may “improve” your prose in ways that change your meaning or flatten your voice. A few guidelines:

Always review changes individually. Do not accept a batch rewrite. Ask for a list of specific suggestions, not a new version of the paragraph.
Reject stylistic changes that do not suit you. LLMs tend toward a generic, slightly formal tone. If the model suggests replacing your phrasing with something blander, keep yours.
Watch for meaning drift. The model may tighten a sentence in a way that subtly changes what you are saying. Read each suggestion against your intended meaning.
Do not let it restructure your argument. If you ask for “clarity” suggestions and the model proposes reordering your paragraphs, that is an intellectual decision, not a copy-editing one. Make those choices yourself.

title: Copy Editing
text:
What It Does Well

Catching typos and grammatical errors, including subtle ones (subject-verb agreement across long sentences, misplaced modifiers)
Identifying repetitive word choice
Flagging inconsistent formatting (dates, capitalization, hyphenation)
Spotting missing articles or prepositions that a spell-checker would miss

title: Copy Editing
text:
What It Does Poorly

Recognizing intentional stylistic choices (sentence fragments for emphasis, deliberate repetition, rhetorical structure)
Understanding disciplinary conventions (Chicago-style footnotes, the use of the historical present, block quotation formatting)
Knowing when not to change something

title: Copy Editing
text:
A Practical Workflow

Write your draft yourself. Do not use GenAI for initial drafting.
Revise your draft yourself. Get the argument and structure where you want them.
Run a proofreading pass with the LLM, asking for a list of mechanical errors.
Accept or reject each suggestion individually.
Optionally, run a clarity pass — again reviewing each suggestion against your original intent.
Read the final version yourself. You are responsible for every word.

This workflow keeps you in the author’s seat throughout. The LLM functions as a very fast, very patient proofreader — nothing more.

title: Limitations and Risks
text: The preceding pages describe what GenAI tools can do. This section describes what they cannot do, what they do badly, and where using them can lead you astray. For a historian, understanding the failure modes is not optional — it is the difference between a useful tool and a liability.

title: Limitations and Risks
text: The preceding pages describe what GenAI tools can do. This section describes what they cannot do, what they do badly, and where using them can lead you astray. For a historian, understanding the failure modes is not optional — it is the difference between a useful tool and a liability.

title: Limitations and Risks
text:
Hallucination
This is the most important limitation. LLMs generate text that is statistically plausible, not text that is true. They will:

Invent citations. They produce author names, titles, journal names, volume numbers, and page ranges that look entirely real but correspond to no actual publication. This is not rare; it happens routinely.
Fabricate historical details. They may generate plausible-sounding dates, names, or events that did not occur, especially for obscure topics.
Blend real and false information seamlessly. A paragraph might contain three accurate claims and one fabricated one, with no difference in tone or confidence.


What to Do About It

Verify every factual claim against a source you trust.
Verify every citation in a library catalogue, JSTOR, or Google Scholar.
Never cite an LLM as a source in scholarly work. It is not a source; it is a text-generation engine.
Be especially cautious about topics that are obscure, recent, or poorly represented in English-language digital text.

title: Limitations and Risks
text:
Bias and Representation
LLMs reflect the biases of their training data. For historians, this has specific implications:

Anglophone and Western bias. The models know more about the history of Britain and the United States than about the history of, say, the Swahili coast or Central Asia. Responses about less-represented topics will be thinner, more generic, and more likely to contain errors.
Historiographic lag. The training data reflects the published literature, which means the models tend to reproduce older historiographic framings. They may default to nationalist narratives, Great Man history, or other interpretive frameworks that the discipline has moved beyond.
Erasure and euphemism. Some models are tuned to avoid controversial topics, which can result in euphemistic or evasive language about slavery, colonialism, genocide, and other subjects that historians must discuss directly.


What to Do About It

Treat LLM output as you would an encyclopedia entry: a starting point that reflects a particular perspective, not a neutral authority.
Be alert to gaps — what the model does not say can be as revealing as what it does.
If you notice the model using euphemistic language about sensitive historical topics, push back explicitly in your prompt: “Please use direct, scholarly language. Do not euphemize.”

title: Limitations and Risks
text:
The Illusion of Understanding
LLMs produce text that reads as if it were written by someone who understands the topic. This is misleading. The model does not understand; it predicts. The practical consequence is that:

It cannot distinguish between a strong argument and a weak one.
It cannot judge the reliability of a source.
It cannot weigh conflicting evidence.
It cannot tell you when a question is more complex than it appears.

These are the tasks that define historical practice. They cannot be delegated to an LLM.

title: Limitations and Risks
text:
Privacy and Intellectual Property

Your Data
When you paste text into a web-based chatbot, that text is transmitted to the company’s servers. Consider:

Unpublished research. If you paste a chapter draft into ChatGPT, that text has left your control. Most services state that they do not use paid-tier conversations for training, but policies vary and change.
Sensitive data. Records involving living people, embargoed materials, or data covered by research ethics agreements should not be pasted into commercial LLM tools without careful consideration.
Student work. If you are using GenAI tools to evaluate student submissions, be aware that you may be sharing student work with a third-party service.



The Model’s “Knowledge”
LLMs were trained on text scraped from the internet, including copyrighted material. The legal and ethical status of this training is unresolved and actively litigated. This is worth being aware of, though it does not directly affect most research use cases.

title: Limitations and Risks
text:
Over-Reliance
The subtlest risk is not that the tool will fail dramatically but that it will work well enough to change your behaviour. Historians who use LLMs for translation may gradually do less of their own translation practice. Historians who use LLMs for literature discovery may search databases less thoroughly. Historians who use LLMs for coding may understand their own scripts less deeply.
None of these are inevitable outcomes, but they are real risks. The best safeguard is intentionality: decide in advance what you want to learn and what you want to delegate, and revisit that decision periodically.

title: Limitations and Risks
text:
Summary

Summary of limitations and mitigations






Risk
Mitigation




Hallucinated citations
Verify every reference in a catalogue or database


Fabricated facts
Check all claims against trusted sources


Bias and gaps
Treat output as a starting point, not an authority


Privacy concerns
Do not paste sensitive or unpublished material into free-tier tools


Over-reliance
Decide consciously what to delegate; maintain your own skills


Illusion of understanding
Reserve interpretation, judgment, and argument for yourself

title: Limitations and Risks
text:
The Bottom Line
These tools are useful. They are also unreliable, biased, and incapable of the interpretive work that defines our discipline. Using them well means using them for what they are good at (mechanical tasks, pattern recognition, draft editing) while keeping the intellectual work — the reading, the thinking, the arguing — firmly in your own hands.
That is not a limitation of the technology. It is the point.

title: GenAI for Historians
text: This guide is for historians — faculty and graduate students — who are curious about generative AI tools but have not yet used them, or who have tried them briefly and want to understand what they can realistically do for historical research. No technical background is assumed. You do not need to know how to code, and you do not need to spend any money to get started.

title: GenAI for Historians
text: This guide is for historians — faculty and graduate students — who are curious about generative AI tools but have not yet used them, or who have tried them briefly and want to understand what they can realistically do for historical research. No technical background is assumed. You do not need to know how to code, and you do not need to spend any money to get started.

title: GenAI for Historians
text:
What This Guide Is Not
This is not an argument that you should use these tools. It is not a technology manifesto or a prediction about the future of the discipline. Some historians will find GenAI useful for specific research tasks. Others will try it and decide it does not suit their workflow. Both are reasonable outcomes.
The goal is simply to give you enough hands-on experience to make an informed judgment.

title: GenAI for Historians
text:
How It Is Organized
The guide moves from free, low-commitment starting points to more advanced (and more expensive) tools:

What Are LLMs? — A brief, non-technical explanation of how these tools work, what they are good at, and why they make mistakes. Understanding the basics helps you use them more effectively and spot their failures.
Getting Started — How to begin with freely available tools (ChatGPT, Google Gemini) today, with no account setup beyond an email address. Includes a first exercise you can try in ten minutes.
Research Tasks — Concrete examples of how historians are using GenAI for transcription, translation, literature discovery, and coding. Each section includes sample prompts you can adapt to your own work.
Deep Research Tools — Several products now offer automated literature review: they search the web, read sources, and produce structured reports with citations. These are powerful and impressive — and they are also where GenAI’s tendency to hallucinate becomes most dangerous. This section explains what they do well and how to avoid being misled.
Case Studies — Two projects built by a historian using these tools: re-OCR’ing and building a searchable knowledge graph of 8,128 Royal Society articles (1665–1869), and transcribing and visualizing an archival collection of Joseph Banks’ correspondence on the British leather trade. Neither required advanced programming skills.
LLMs and Teaching — Why prohibition is difficult to enforce, why AI detection tools are unreliable (and biased), and what actually works: process-based assessment, Google Docs version history, assignment design, oral verification, and the ownership model for courses that move beyond prohibition.
Copy Editing — Using GenAI to proofread and polish your own drafted prose — what it does well, where it over-corrects, and how to stay in control of your voice.
Going Further — When free tools hit their limits and paid subscriptions become worthwhile. An honest look at costs, capabilities, and what the more advanced tools (Claude Pro, ChatGPT Plus, Claude Code) actually offer.
Limitations — What these tools get wrong, how to recognize it, and what you should never trust them to do unsupervised. This section is as important as any of the others.

title: GenAI for Historians
section: A Note on Tools and Vendors
text:
A Note on Tools and Vendors
This guide mentions specific products by name (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) because it is impossible to give practical advice without doing so. Naming a tool is not an endorsement. The landscape is changing rapidly, and the specific products available when you read this may differ from those available when it was written. The underlying skills — writing effective prompts, verifying outputs, knowing when to trust and when to doubt — transfer across tools.

title: GenAI for Historians
text:
Getting Started
If you have fifteen minutes and want to try something right now, go to Getting Started.

title: LLMs and Teaching
text:
Three years after ChatGPT’s release, the evidence is clear: most university students use GenAI tools regularly, regardless of course policies. Surveys across multiple countries and institutions consistently find usage rates above 80%. A 2025 study at Middlebury College found that over 80% of students used GenAI for coursework — one of the fastest technology adoption rates ever documented in higher education. A UK survey the same year put the figure at 92% of undergraduates using AI in some aspect of academic work.
This does not mean every student is using GenAI to cheat. Most use it for legitimate tasks — explaining concepts, brainstorming, grammar checking. But it does mean that prohibition, as a policy, exists in tension with the reality of student behaviour. Faculty who choose prohibition need to understand both why it is worth attempting and why it is difficult to enforce, so they can design their courses accordingly.
This section is addressed to faculty in all positions on the spectrum: those who want to prohibit GenAI use, those who want to permit it cautiously, and those still deciding. The goal is practical advice grounded in what we have learned since 2023.

title: LLMs and Teaching
text:
Three years after ChatGPT’s release, the evidence is clear: most university students use GenAI tools regularly, regardless of course policies. Surveys across multiple countries and institutions consistently find usage rates above 80%. A 2025 study at Middlebury College found that over 80% of students used GenAI for coursework — one of the fastest technology adoption rates ever documented in higher education. A UK survey the same year put the figure at 92% of undergraduates using AI in some aspect of academic work.
This does not mean every student is using GenAI to cheat. Most use it for legitimate tasks — explaining concepts, brainstorming, grammar checking. But it does mean that prohibition, as a policy, exists in tension with the reality of student behaviour. Faculty who choose prohibition need to understand both why it is worth attempting and why it is difficult to enforce, so they can design their courses accordingly.
This section is addressed to faculty in all positions on the spectrum: those who want to prohibit GenAI use, those who want to permit it cautiously, and those still deciding. The goal is practical advice grounded in what we have learned since 2023.

title: LLMs and Teaching
text:
Why Prohibition Is Difficult
Prohibition is a legitimate pedagogical choice, particularly in courses designed to develop foundational writing and research skills. But it faces several structural challenges:
Ubiquitous access. GenAI tools are free, require only a web browser, and are increasingly integrated into the software students already use — word processors, search engines, email clients, and phones. There is no way to prevent access outside the classroom, and increasingly little way to prevent it inside the classroom.
Unreliable detection. AI detection tools (Turnitin’s AI detector, GPTZero, Originality.ai, and others) do not work reliably enough to serve as evidence of misconduct. Independent testing has found false positive rates far higher than vendors claim, and the tools are systematically biased against non-native English speakers and neurodivergent students. A growing number of universities — including Vanderbilt, Johns Hopkins, UCLA, the University of Waterloo, Michigan State, and the University of Pittsburgh — have disabled Turnitin’s AI detection feature entirely. Several students have successfully sued or settled after false accusations based on AI detector output.
Evasion tools. A thriving industry of “humanizer” tools (Undetectable.ai, StealthWriter, StealthGPT, and many others) rewrites AI-generated text to evade detection software. These tools are cheap or free, easy to use, and specifically marketed to students. Turnitin announced “AI bypasser detection” in August 2025, but this creates an arms race that detection is unlikely to win. The existence of these tools means that even if detection software were accurate — which it is not — determined students could circumvent it.
Chilling effect. Surveys find that roughly half of students report being afraid to use AI in any capacity for fear of false accusations, even in courses where some use is permitted. This punishes cautious, honest students while doing little to deter deliberate misuse.
The fairness problem. This is perhaps the most corrosive consequence of unenforced prohibition, and it deserves frank acknowledgement. When a significant number of students use GenAI to polish their writing while others do not, the overall quality of submissions rises. Faculty may not consciously curve grades, but they calibrate expectations against what they see. A student who writes competently but with occasional passive voice, awkward transitions, or poorly structured paragraphs — the normal imperfections of developing writers — will appear weaker relative to a cohort whose prose has been quietly smoothed by an LLM. The honest student’s B+ becomes a B; the AI-polished student’s B+ becomes an A-.
Students notice this. They see roommates and friends submit AI-assisted work and receive high marks. The message is clear: the system rewards those who use GenAI and punishes those who do not, regardless of what the syllabus says. This breeds cynicism, erodes trust in the instructor, and creates pressure on honest students to start using the tools themselves — not because they want to, but because they feel they have no choice.
This dynamic is one of the strongest arguments for moving beyond prohibition where possible, or at least for coupling prohibition with assessment methods (oral exams, in-class writing, process documentation) that cannot be gamed in this way. If the grade reflects demonstrated understanding rather than prose quality alone, the advantage of AI-polished text largely disappears.
None of this means prohibition is impossible. It means that prohibition enforced primarily through detection is a losing strategy, and that unenforced prohibition creates its own injustices. Effective prohibition requires a different approach.

title: LLMs and Teaching
section: The Problem with AI Detection Tools
text:
The Problem with AI Detection Tools
This deserves its own section because faculty may be under the impression that detection tools are a viable safeguard. They are not.

False Positives
AI detection tools work by measuring statistical features of text — perplexity (how surprising the word choices are) and burstiness (how much sentence length and structure vary). Human writing tends to be more variable; AI-generated text tends to be more uniform. But these are tendencies, not rules, and the overlap is large.
Independent research has documented:

False positive rates between 5% and 20% on general student submissions, far higher than the <1% vendors claim.
A Washington Post test that produced a 50% false positive rate on certain content using Turnitin’s detector.
Accuracy that drops to 80–85% when text has been lightly paraphrased or edited — which is how most students actually use GenAI.



Bias Against Non-Native English Speakers
This is the most serious problem. A widely cited 2023 study found that AI detectors misclassified over 61% of essays by non-native English speakers as AI-generated, while achieving near-perfect accuracy on native English speaker essays. A follow-up found that 97% of TOEFL essays were flagged by at least one detector.
The reason is structural: detectors penalize simpler vocabulary, shorter sentences, and more formulaic structures — precisely the features of competent second-language academic writing. This is not a bug that can be patched; it is inherent to how the technology works.
In 2025, multiple institutions reported cases of international students accused of AI cheating based on detector output that was later shown to be wrong. A 2025 study found that Black students were disproportionately accused, with 20% reporting false accusations compared to 7% of white students.


Bias Against Neurodivergent Students
Students on the autism spectrum often produce writing that is highly structured, literal, and consistent in tone — features that overlap with the statistical signatures detectors associate with AI. Students with ADHD who write in focused bursts can produce similar patterns. Documented cases include students falsely accused who had to disclose their diagnoses to clear their names.


The Institutional Response
The American Historical Association published its Guiding Principles for Artificial Intelligence in History Education in August 2025, reflecting a two-year process of consultation. The AHA emphasizes that GenAI “can produce material that appears polished and credible, but assessing their outputs demands critical skills that the models themselves can neither teach nor foster.” The framing is disciplinary rather than technological: the question is what historical training requires, not what detection tools can do.
A growing consensus among teaching and learning centres holds that detection-based enforcement is unsustainable. As Inside Higher Ed reported in December 2025: “You can’t AI-proof the classroom, experts say. Get creative instead.”

title: LLMs and Teaching
text:
Best Practices for Prohibition
If you choose to prohibit GenAI use in your courses — and there are good reasons to do so, especially in first-year writing courses — the following practices are more effective than relying on detection software.

In-Class Writing
The most straightforward safeguard is supervised writing. If a significant assessment takes place in the classroom, GenAI cannot be used. Options include:

In-class essay exams (open-book or closed-book).
Supervised writing sessions where students work on drafts during class time.
In-class primary source analysis exercises.

Limitations: In-class writing tests speed as well as understanding, disadvantages students who need accommodations, and cannot be the sole assessment method for courses with substantial research components.


Process-Based Assessment
Rather than evaluating only the final product, require evidence of the writing process:

Staged submissions. Require an annotated outline, a rough draft, and a revised draft at intervals across the term. Each stage should show genuine evolution — new ideas appearing, arguments being restructured, evidence being added or reconsidered.
Research logs. Have students maintain logs of sources consulted, search strategies used, and how their argument developed. These are difficult to fabricate convincingly because they require the kind of specific, idiosyncratic detail that GenAI does not produce well.
Reflective annotations. Ask students to annotate their own drafts, explaining why they made specific argumentative choices, where they struggled, and what they would change.



Google Docs Version History
Google Docs automatically saves a timestamped record of every addition, deletion, and edit. If you require students to compose their work in a shared Google Doc (with the instructor added as a viewer), the version history provides an evidence trail of the writing process.
What it reveals:

Typing patterns. Genuine student writing shows incremental word-by-word and sentence-by-sentence additions, with pauses, deletions, false starts, and revisions.
Copy-paste events. AI-assisted work often appears as large blocks of text pasted in whole, with minimal subsequent editing. This is visible in the revision history.
Time spent. Total editing time and the number of sessions are logged. A 3,000-word essay that appears fully formed in a single 20-minute session is a different object than one built over five sessions across two weeks.
Editing trajectory. You can see whether ideas evolved organically or appeared fully formed.

Tools that enhance this: Browser extensions like Revision History (used by over 200,000 teachers) provide video replay of the writing process and highlight large paste/deletion events. Draftback offers similar functionality.
Limitations: A determined student could type AI-generated text character by character, though this is time-consuming enough to be largely self-defeating. Students who draft in another application and paste will be visible in the history, which itself becomes a point of conversation.
Implementation: State in your syllabus that all written work must be composed in a shared Google Doc. Explain that you may review the version history. This is transparent, does not rely on detection software, and provides a basis for conversation rather than accusation.


Assignment Design That Resists GenAI
Some assignments are harder to complete with GenAI than others. For history courses:

Specific primary source analysis. Require analysis of particular pages or passages from specific archival collections, especially unpublished or poorly digitized materials unlikely to be in LLM training data. “Analyse folios 12–15 of the York Factory post journal for 1821” is harder to outsource than “Discuss the fur trade in western Canada.”
Personal engagement with sources. Ask students to reflect on their experience navigating a finding aid, visiting an archive, or encountering a physical document. “Describe what surprised you about the condition of the original manuscript” cannot be answered by a tool that has never seen the manuscript.
Course-specific content. Require engagement with specific in-class discussions, guest lectures, or seminar debates. “How does Thompson’s argument about the moral economy relate to the point raised in seminar about the Saskatchewan wheat pools?” ties the assignment to a conversation the student had to be present for.
Local and obscure topics. LLMs perform poorly on topics that are underrepresented in their training data. Saskatchewan local history, small-town newspapers, and understudied archival collections are natural territory for assignments that GenAI cannot handle well.
Multi-source synthesis. Require students to connect three or four specific primary sources in ways that demand genuine analytical work. The more specific and idiosyncratic the sources, the harder the task is to outsource.



Oral Verification
A short oral conversation about a written assignment is the single most effective safeguard against work a student does not understand. This can take several forms:

Oral exam on a research paper (15–20 minutes). The student discusses their argument, evidence, and research process. The grade reflects demonstrated understanding, not performance anxiety. One model already in use: “If you do the work yourself and write your paper, this will be a low-stress discussion of your evidence and arguments.”
Brief follow-up conversations (5–10 minutes). After returning a graded assignment, meet individually with students to discuss their work. This need not be framed as verification; it can be framed as feedback.
In-class presentations. Students present their research to the class and field questions. This is standard seminar pedagogy and serves double duty as GenAI verification.

Resource implications: Oral verification is time-intensive. A 15-minute conversation with each student in a class of 30 requires 7.5 hours of faculty time. This is feasible for seminars and upper-level courses but challenging for large lecture sections without TA support.

title: LLMs and Teaching
text:
Moving Beyond Prohibition
Some faculty will conclude that prohibition, while legitimate, is not the right approach for their courses — particularly at the upper-undergraduate or graduate level. The following practices allow GenAI use while maintaining rigorous standards.

The Ownership Model
The most effective framework centres on ownership: students are intellectually responsible for every word they submit, regardless of how it was produced. GenAI may be used for specific tasks (literature review, transcription, copy editing, testing ideas), but the student must be able to explain, defend, and contextualize every claim in their work.
Under this model:

The syllabus specifies what GenAI use is permitted and what is not.
Students disclose any GenAI use.
An oral component (exam, defence, follow-up conversation) verifies understanding.
Work that the student cannot defend does not receive credit.
Students are advised that completing the core writing themselves is the safest approach.

This shifts the enforcement mechanism from “did you use AI?” (which cannot be reliably determined) to “do you understand your own work?” (which can be tested directly through conversation).


Disclosure Requirements
Where GenAI use is permitted, require disclosure. This normalizes transparency and makes concealment — rather than use — the integrity violation.

State in the syllabus exactly what must be disclosed and how.
Provide examples: “I used ChatGPT to identify relevant secondary literature, then verified each citation in JSTOR” or “I used Claude to help debug a Python script for data cleaning.”
Distinguish between incidental use (spell-check, grammar tools) and substantive use (drafting, research, analysis).



AI as Object of Study
One approach that serves both pedagogy and AI literacy: make GenAI output something students analyse critically rather than something they produce with.

Fact-checking exercises. Have students ask an LLM to summarize a historical debate, then identify what it gets wrong, what perspectives it omits, and what sources it fabricates. This teaches source criticism applied to a new kind of text.
Comparative analysis. Students write their own analysis of a primary source, then generate an LLM analysis of the same source, then write a critical comparison. Where does the LLM miss nuance? Where does it hallucinate context? What does it do well?
Historiographic audit. Ask an LLM to produce a reading list on a topic the student knows well. Have them evaluate it: which sources are real? Which are fabricated? Which are accurately characterized? This is a powerful exercise in bibliographic literacy.

These assignments cannot be completed by GenAI because the task is to evaluate GenAI’s limitations. They teach the critical skills students need whether or not they use these tools in their own research.

title: LLMs and Teaching
text:
What We Know Works
After three years of institutional experimentation, a rough consensus is emerging:

Detection tools are not the answer. They produce too many false positives, are biased against vulnerable student populations, and are defeated by freely available evasion tools. Multiple institutions have disabled them. Do not build your integrity strategy around detection.
Assessment design is the answer. Assignments that require demonstrated understanding — through oral components, process documentation, in-class work, or engagement with specific and idiosyncratic sources — are more robust than any technological fix.
Clarity is essential. Students report confusion about what is permitted. Every syllabus should state the course’s GenAI policy explicitly, including what is allowed, what is prohibited, and what must be disclosed.
Instructor autonomy matters. Different courses have different goals. A first-year writing course and a fourth-year research seminar warrant different policies. Faculty should be supported in making the choice that serves their students and their pedagogy.
The conversation is ongoing. No policy adopted today will be the right policy in three years. Build in review mechanisms and be willing to adjust.

title: LLMs and Teaching
text:
Further Reading

American Historical Association, Guiding Principles for Artificial Intelligence in History Education (August 2025).
Liang, W., et al. “GPT Detectors Are Biased Against Non-Native English Writers.” Patterns 4, no. 7 (2023). PMC.
Inside Higher Ed, “You Can’t AI-Proof the Classroom, Experts Say. Get Creative Instead” (December 2025).
NBC News, “To Avoid Accusations of AI Cheating, College Students Are Turning to AI” (2025).

title: Case Studies
text:
The earlier sections of this guide describe individual tasks — transcription, translation, coding, copy editing. But the real power of GenAI tools emerges when you combine them into a sustained research workflow. The two projects described here were built by a historian, not a computer scientist, using the kinds of tools discussed in this guide. Neither required advanced programming skills at the outset. Both started with a straightforward question and grew as the tools made each next step feasible.
The point is not that every historian should build a knowledge graph. It is that these tools lower the barrier to computational research methods enough that projects which once required a dedicated digital humanities team can now be undertaken by a single researcher with a clear question and a willingness to learn.

title: Case Studies
text:
The earlier sections of this guide describe individual tasks — transcription, translation, coding, copy editing. But the real power of GenAI tools emerges when you combine them into a sustained research workflow. The two projects described here were built by a historian, not a computer scientist, using the kinds of tools discussed in this guide. Neither required advanced programming skills at the outset. Both started with a straightforward question and grew as the tools made each next step feasible.
The point is not that every historian should build a knowledge graph. It is that these tools lower the barrier to computational research methods enough that projects which once required a dedicated digital humanities team can now be undertaken by a single researcher with a clear question and a willingness to learn.

title: Case Studies
section: Case Study 1: The Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society (1665–1869)
text:
Case Study 1: The Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society (1665–1869)

The Question
The Philosophical Transactions is the world’s first and longest-running scientific journal, published continuously since 1665. Its 8,128 articles and 106,829 pages document over two centuries of scientific thought in Britain and beyond — from Newton’s optics to Darwin’s early papers, from earthquake reports to experiments on air pressure. The full run is digitized and available through JSTOR’s Early Journal Content on the Internet Archive. But how do you actually use 200 years of scientific literature as a historical source? You cannot read 107,000 pages. Keyword search misses vocabulary drift (“electrical fluid” for electricity, “natural philosophy” for science). And the connections between articles — the people, the places, the specimens, the ideas — are invisible in a flat text archive.


What Was Built
The project proceeded in stages, each one made possible by GenAI tools:
Stage 1: Re-OCR the entire collection. The existing digitizations had mediocre OCR, particularly for early volumes with long-s typography and Latin text. Using OLMoCR (an AI-powered OCR system) running on H100 GPUs at a university cluster, all 8,128 articles were re-processed. The result: 197 MB of clean, searchable text, with language detection (English, Latin, French) and table identification. Processing took roughly a day of cluster time.
Stage 2: Extract entities. Using an LLM (Gemini), the project extracted people, places, and scientific taxa (species names) mentioned across the entire corpus. The result: over 86,000 entity mentions — 36,824 people, 32,527 places, and 17,122 scientific taxa — linked back to the specific articles and pages where they appear.
Stage 3: Build a knowledge graph. The extracted entities were assembled into a network: nodes for people, places, taxa, and articles, with edges representing co-occurrence (who is mentioned alongside whom) and document membership (which entities appear in which article). The graph contains 94,601 nodes and over a million edges. A temporal layer links articles to years, decades, and centuries, enabling queries like “show me all articles about electricity published between 1740 and 1760.”
Stage 4: Create searchable embeddings. Every page of every article was converted into a numerical vector (an “embedding”) that captures its semantic content. This enables similarity search: you can ask for pages that are about a topic, not just pages that contain a specific word. The vector index holds 92,786 page embeddings.
Stage 5: Build a query interface. The knowledge graph and vector index were combined into a GraphRAG (Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation) system. A researcher types a natural language question. The system searches the graph for relevant entities and the vector index for semantically similar pages, retrieves the actual text of the most relevant articles, and uses an LLM to synthesize a response grounded in the primary sources, with full citations.
Stage 6: Publish the archive. All 8,128 articles were published as a browsable, searchable static website with downloadable Markdown and JSON files for every article.


What It Looks Like in Practice
A historian studying Joseph Banks’ influence on botanical exploration types:

What role did Joseph Banks play in directing botanical research through the Royal Society?

The system searches the entity graph for Banks, finds the 1,599 pages that mention him, retrieves the most relevant articles, and generates a synthesized response drawing on dozens of primary sources — with clickable citations to each article. The entire process takes about 20 seconds.
A historian of electricity types:

How did understandings of electricity change between 1700 and 1800?

The system finds articles discussing electrical phenomena across the century, accounts for vocabulary change (it knows that “electrical fluid,” “Leyden jar,” and “galvanism” are all relevant), and produces a narrative overview grounded in the actual articles, not in the LLM’s general knowledge.


The Tools Used








Task
Tool
Difficulty




OCR processing
OLMoCR on university GPU cluster
Moderate (required cluster access)


Entity extraction
Gemini API (LLM)
Low (write a prompt, run a script)


Knowledge graph
NetworkX (Python library)
Low-moderate (LLM wrote most of the code)


Vector embeddings
NVIDIA NV-Embed-v2
Moderate (requires GPU)


Query system
Python + Gemini API
Low (LLM assisted with code)


Static website
Jekyll + Pagefind
Low


Deployment
Modal.com (serverless)
Low-moderate



The historian who built this project is not a software engineer. Most of the Python code was written with LLM assistance — describing what was needed, reviewing what the LLM produced, testing it on the data, and iterating. The skills required were: a clear research question, the ability to describe data clearly, and the patience to verify that each stage worked correctly before moving to the next.


What It Made Possible
The project has already supported research on ecological constraints in early modern science, the economic botany of the British Empire, and the history of tanning materials — topics that would have required months of manual searching to investigate across a corpus this large. The GraphRAG system does not replace close reading. It tells the historian which articles to read closely, and it surfaces connections between articles that no individual reader would notice across 8,128 documents.
The entire archive is publicly accessible, and the query interface costs roughly three cents per query to operate.

title: Case Studies
section: Case Study 2: The Banks Archive — Transcribing and Visualizing an Archival Collection
text:
Case Study 2: The Banks Archive — Transcribing and Visualizing an Archival Collection

The Question
Sir Joseph Banks (1743–1820) was president of the Royal Society for over 40 years and one of the most connected figures in British science. His correspondence — thousands of letters spanning botany, exploration, colonial administration, and industrial development — is scattered across archives worldwide. One collection at the Sutro Library in San Francisco contains letters focused on a specific episode: the British leather trade crisis of the late eighteenth century, when oak bark shortages threatened the tanning industry and Banks mobilized his scientific network to find botanical alternatives from across the Empire.
The letters were digitized but not transcribed. Handwritten correspondence from the 1790s is not searchable. The question was: could GenAI tools turn a collection of page images into a searchable, queryable, visualizable research resource?


What Was Built
Stage 1: Image processing and transcription. The digitized letter images were preprocessed (rotation correction, contrast enhancement) and then transcribed using an LLM with vision capabilities. The model was given context about the collection — the era, the handwriting conventions, the subject matter — which significantly improved accuracy. The result: 181 transcribed documents, ready for analysis.
Stage 2: Entity extraction and knowledge graph. Using the same LLM-assisted approach as the Royal Society project, the transcriptions were processed to extract people, places, commodities, and institutions. These were loaded into a Neo4j knowledge graph with 3,200 nodes and 5,300 relationships. The graph captures who wrote to whom, what commodities were discussed, which institutions were involved, and when.
Stage 3: Enrichment from linked data. The knowledge graph was enriched by connecting its entities to external databases:

Wikidata: Biographical data for 76 identified correspondents (birth/death dates, occupations, institutional affiliations).
VIAF (Virtual International Authority File): 735 published works linked to people in the network.
GBIF (Global Biodiversity Information Facility): 215 botanical specimens collected by members of Banks’ network, with geographic coordinates.
Historical trade data: 129 records of British tannin imports from 1800–1844, showing the commercial impact of the botanical alternatives Banks’ network identified.

Stage 4: Interactive visualizations. Five interactive visualizations were generated from the knowledge graph:

A timeline-network showing the leather crisis unfolding from 1773 to 1810, with Banks’ correspondence network layered on top — revealing how scientific knowledge, commercial interests, and parliamentary reform intersected.
A correspondence network mapping the top 120 correspondents as a force-directed graph, showing the structure of Banks’ intellectual community.
A correspondence flow diagram (Sankey) showing who wrote to Banks and who Banks wrote to, organized by decade, revealing asymmetries in the network.
A topic-commodity-place flow showing what was discussed, what materials were mentioned, and where they came from, across decades.
A temporal network anchoring people to the years of their correspondence, showing how the network evolved over time.



What It Looks Like in Practice
A historian studying the relationship between science and commerce in the late eighteenth century can now:

Search the transcribed letters for any mention of a specific commodity (cutch, valonia, oak bark) and see every letter that discusses it, with dates and correspondents.
Visualize Banks’ network of correspondents on the leather question and identify who the key intermediaries were between scientific institutions and commercial interests.
Trace how knowledge about a specific tanning material (say, Indian cutch) moved from botanical collectors in Bengal through Banks’ correspondence to commercial adoption in London, with dates at every step.
Cross-reference the correspondence with actual import data to see whether Banks’ advocacy had measurable commercial effects.

None of this was possible before the letters were transcribed. The transcription alone — turning page images into searchable text — transformed the collection from an archival curiosity into a usable research resource.
