The Tools Used








Task
Tool
Difficulty




Image preprocessing
Python (rotation, contrast)
Low (LLM wrote the scripts)


Transcription
LLM with vision (Gemini)
Low (upload images, provide context)


Entity extraction
LLM (Gemini API)
Low


Knowledge graph
Neo4j + Python
Moderate (LLM assisted with Cypher queries)


Linked data enrichment
Wikidata, VIAF, GBIF APIs
Low-moderate (LLM wrote API scripts)


Vector embeddings
Gemini embeddings (3072-dim)
Low


Visualizations
D3.js, vis.js (generated by Python scripts)
Moderate (LLM wrote visualization code)





What It Made Possible
The project turned an untranscribed archival collection into a research infrastructure. The visualizations revealed structural features of Banks’ network that are invisible in the letters themselves — particularly the role of intermediary figures who connected scientific, commercial, and governmental spheres. The trade data integration showed that the botanical alternatives Banks’ network identified did enter British commerce at scale, a finding that would have required painstaking manual cross-referencing without the knowledge graph.

title: Case Studies
section: What These Projects Have in Common
text:
What These Projects Have in Common
Both projects share a pattern that is worth highlighting:

The historian drove the research questions. The tools did not generate the questions; they made it feasible to pursue questions that would otherwise have been impractical.
GenAI was used for mechanical tasks, not interpretation. Transcription, entity extraction, code generation, data transformation — these are the tasks the tools handled. The analysis, the arguments, and the historical judgments remained with the researcher.
Each stage was built incrementally. Neither project was planned as a monolith. The historian started with a simple task (transcribe some letters; re-OCR some articles), verified the results, and then asked: “What can I do with this now?” Each stage suggested the next.
The LLM wrote most of the code. The researcher described what was needed, the LLM produced Python scripts, and the researcher tested and refined them. This is the coding workflow described in the Research Tasks section, applied at scale.
Verification was constant. Transcriptions were checked against originals. Extracted entities were reviewed. Code was tested on real data. The tools accelerated the work; they did not eliminate the need for critical judgment.
The results are open and reusable. Both projects produced publicly accessible resources — a searchable archive, interactive visualizations, downloadable data — that other researchers can build on.


What Was Actually Hard
It would be misleading to suggest these projects were effortless. The genuinely difficult parts were:

Getting access to compute resources for large-scale OCR and embedding generation (university cluster access, or paid cloud GPU time).
Data cleaning — historical data is messy, inconsistent, and full of edge cases that no tool handles automatically.
Verification at scale — checking thousands of transcriptions and entity extractions for accuracy is time-consuming even when the error rate is low.
Learning enough about the tools to describe what you want effectively. The LLM writes better code when you can describe your data clearly.

What was not hard, and would have been prohibitively hard five years ago: the transcription itself, the entity extraction, the code generation, the visualization, and the construction of a queryable knowledge graph. These are the tasks that GenAI tools have made accessible to historians working without dedicated technical support.

title: What Are Large Language Models?
text:
Large language models (LLMs) are software systems trained on enormous quantities of text. When you type a prompt, the model generates a response by predicting what text is most likely to follow, drawing on patterns learned during training. The result often reads like it was written by a knowledgeable human — but the process behind it is fundamentally different from human thought.
Understanding this distinction matters. It explains both why these tools are useful and why they fail in characteristic ways.

title: What Are Large Language Models?
text:
Large language models (LLMs) are software systems trained on enormous quantities of text. When you type a prompt, the model generates a response by predicting what text is most likely to follow, drawing on patterns learned during training. The result often reads like it was written by a knowledgeable human — but the process behind it is fundamentally different from human thought.
Understanding this distinction matters. It explains both why these tools are useful and why they fail in characteristic ways.

title: What Are Large Language Models?
text:
How They Work (Without the Jargon)
Imagine you had read every book, article, and webpage published in English (and many other languages) up to a certain date. You would develop a powerful sense of what kinds of sentences follow other sentences — what arguments historians tend to make about the Atlantic slave trade, what a colonial office list looks like, how a French land grant is typically structured.
An LLM has something like that statistical sense, but without understanding. It does not know that the Treaty of Paris was signed in 1763 the way you know it. It has learned that in texts about the Seven Years’ War, the phrase “Treaty of Paris” is very often followed by “1763.” This is a crucial distinction.
When the pattern is strong and well-represented in the training data, the model produces accurate, useful output. When the pattern is weak, ambiguous, or absent, the model produces confident-sounding nonsense. It does not know the difference.

title: What Are Large Language Models?
text:
What They Are Good At
LLMs excel at tasks where pattern recognition is the core skill:

Transcription assistance: Recognizing words in difficult handwriting or degraded print, especially when given context about the document’s era and language.
Translation: Producing serviceable rough translations, particularly for well-represented languages (French, Spanish, German, Latin).
Summarization: Condensing long texts into shorter summaries (useful for triage, not for close reading).
Code generation: Writing scripts in Python, R, or other languages for data tasks — often faster and more reliably than a historian learning to code from scratch.
Editing and proofreading: Catching typos, grammar errors, and inconsistencies in your drafted text.
Reformatting and structuring: Converting data between formats, extracting structured information from unstructured text.

title: What Are Large Language Models?
text:
What They Are Bad At
LLMs fail predictably in areas that require knowledge they do not have or reasoning they cannot do:

Factual accuracy: They hallucinate — generating plausible-sounding but false claims, including fabricated citations, invented dates, and non-existent sources. This is not a bug that will be fixed; it is an inherent feature of how the technology works.
Archival research: They cannot access unpublished sources, manuscript collections, or anything not in their training data. They have no way to visit an archive.
Interpretation and argument: They can mimic the form of a historical argument but cannot actually weigh evidence, assess significance, or make the judgments that constitute historical analysis.
Counting and arithmetic: Paradoxically, language models are unreliable at basic math and counting tasks.
Knowing what they do not know: They do not flag uncertainty. A fabricated citation is presented with the same confidence as a real one.

title: What Are Large Language Models?
text:
The Key Habit: Verify Everything
The single most important skill in using these tools is verification. Never trust an LLM’s output without checking it against a source you control. This applies especially to:

Citations and bibliographic references (check that the book or article actually exists)
Dates, names, and factual claims
Translations (compare against the original, or a second source)
Code (test it; does it actually do what you asked?)

If this sounds exhausting, it is worth noting that historians already verify sources as a matter of professional practice. The habit transfers directly. The difference is that with an LLM, you must verify the tool as well as the source.

title: What Are Large Language Models?
text:
A Note on Training Data
LLMs are trained on text that existed up to a certain date (the “knowledge cutoff”). They do not have access to the internet in real time unless specifically designed to search the web (some tools offer this as a feature). They reflect the biases, gaps, and emphases of their training data. For historians, this means:

They know more about topics that are well-represented in English-language digital text.
They know less about topics that are primarily documented in archives, rare books, or languages with less digital presence.
They can reproduce historiographic biases present in the literature they were trained on.

None of this is disqualifying, but it means you should treat LLM output the way you would treat any secondary source: as a starting point that requires critical evaluation, not as an authority.
